walkthrough.title: Unfold the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Everdell set up. First, unfold the main game board and place it in the center of the table. This is the beautiful landscape where you will be sending your workers. You will notice several locations printed on the board with open circles where workers can stand. Make sure everyone can reach the board comfortably.

walkthrough.title: Assemble the Ever Tree
walkthrough.text: Now for the showpiece. Find the Ever Tree and assemble it by slotting the cardboard pieces together. Place the completed tree on its designated spot on the board. The Ever Tree has branches at the top where Special Event cards will sit, and it is also where you will track the season your workers unlock. It looks amazing and really sets the mood for the game.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Card Deck
walkthrough.text: Find all one hundred twenty-eight Critter and Construction cards. These have a leaf pattern on the back. Give them a thorough shuffle and place the deck face down near the board. This is the main deck you will draw from throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Meadow
walkthrough.text: Draw eight cards from the top of the deck and lay them face up in a row beside the board. This is called the Meadow, and it acts like a shared market. Any player can play a card from the Meadow on their turn instead of playing from their hand. Whenever a card is taken from the Meadow, immediately replace it with a new card from the deck.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Forest Locations
walkthrough.text: Find the Forest cards. These are the large location tiles that go in the empty spaces along the forest path on the board. For a two-player game, place three Forest cards. For three players, use four. For four players, use all the Forest card spaces. Shuffle them and deal them randomly into the available forest slots. These give you additional worker placement options beyond the basic board locations.

walkthrough.title: Place Special Events on the Ever Tree
walkthrough.text: Find the four Special Event cards. These are the larger cards with unique illustrations. Place them face up on the branches of the Ever Tree. Special Events give bonus points at the end of the game if you meet their requirements. Any player can claim one by using an action, but each event can only be claimed once, so keep an eye on them.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out Basic Events
walkthrough.text: Find the Basic Event tiles. Place them along the bottom of the board in their designated spots. There are always four Basic Events available. Like Special Events, these award bonus points if you meet their conditions, and each can only be claimed by one player. These tend to be simpler to achieve than the Special Events.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Resource Supply
walkthrough.text: Everdell uses four types of resources: twigs, resin, pebbles, and berries. Sort them into separate piles and place them near the board where everyone can reach. Twigs look like little sticks, resin looks like amber drops, pebbles are small stones, and berries are round purple tokens. This shared supply is where you will collect resources from and pay them back to.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Point Tokens
walkthrough.text: Find all the point tokens, which come in values of one, three, and five. Place them in a pile near the resource supply. You will earn these throughout the game from various card abilities and actions. They count toward your final score at the end.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Player Components
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes the matching set of six wooden worker meeples. You also need a spot in front of you for your city, which is where you will play cards. Your city can hold up to fifteen cards total, so leave plenty of table space. Each player also takes a scoring marker if your copy includes them.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Workers
walkthrough.text: Of your six workers, only two are available right now. Place two workers in front of you ready to deploy. The other four go on the Ever Tree in their designated season spots. You will gain one worker when you prepare for spring, one for summer, and two when you prepare for autumn. Workers are the most precious resource in the game, so use them wisely.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Deal five cards from the deck to each player. Look at your cards but keep them hidden from other players. Your hand limit throughout the game is eight cards, so be mindful as you draw more. These starting cards are the seeds of your city, so start thinking about which ones work well together.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to go first. The rulebook suggests the player who most recently visited a forest or nature reserve. Play proceeds clockwise from the first player. In Everdell, players can be in different seasons at different times, so the turn order stays clockwise regardless of season.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: Your table should now have the board with the Ever Tree assembled, eight Meadow cards face up, Forest locations placed, Events ready, resources sorted, and each player with two workers and five cards. Everyone starts in winter with all the basic locations available. Let's learn the rules.

summary.title: Ready to Play

Forest locations, Events, resources, point tokens all placed
Each player: 2 workers ready, 5 cards in hand, winter season

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Everdell is played over four seasons: winter, spring, summer, and autumn. Unlike most games, players do not move through seasons together. You advance to the next season whenever you choose by using the Prepare for Season action, and you might be in summer while another player is still in spring. The game ends when every player has prepared for autumn and used all their workers.

walkthrough.title: Your Three Actions
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you do exactly one of three things. You can Place a Worker on a location to collect resources or trigger an ability. You can Play a Card from your hand or the Meadow into your city. Or you can Prepare for Season to advance to the next season, gain new workers, and activate certain cards. That is it. One action per turn, then play passes clockwise.

walkthrough.title: Action: Place a Worker
walkthrough.text: Take one of your available workers and place it on any open location on the board. Basic locations have an open circle, meaning only one worker can go there at a time. Shared locations have a larger circle and can hold multiple workers. Forest locations work the same way as basic locations. When you place a worker, you immediately gain whatever the location offers, whether that is resources, cards, or something else. Your worker stays on that spot until you Prepare for Season.

walkthrough.title: Board Locations
walkthrough.text: The board has several types of locations. Resource locations give you combinations of twigs, resin, pebbles, or berries. Card locations let you draw cards from the deck or the Meadow. There is a location that lets you discard cards to gain resources. And there are Forest locations that offer unique combinations of resources and cards. Remember, basic locations with an open circle are exclusive, meaning if a worker is already there, nobody else can go until it clears.

walkthrough.title: Action: Play a Card
walkthrough.text: To play a card, choose one from your hand or from the eight face-up Meadow cards. Pay its resource cost shown in the upper left corner, then place it into your city. Your city can hold a maximum of fifteen cards, so choose wisely. There are two categories of cards: Constructions and Critters. Constructions tend to cost twigs, resin, and pebbles. Critters tend to cost berries.

walkthrough.title: Free Critter Plays
walkthrough.text: Here is one of the most important rules in the game. Many Critter cards are linked to a specific Construction card, shown by the icon in the upper left of the Critter card. If you already have that linked Construction in your city, you can play the Critter for free, without paying any berry cost. This only works once per Construction, so place a token or turn the Construction sideways to mark that you have used its free Critter ability. Do not overlook this. Free Critter plays are one of the most powerful things you can do.

walkthrough.title: Unique and Common Cards
walkthrough.text: Cards are marked as either Common or Unique. Common cards have no limit in your city, so you can have multiple copies. Unique cards can only appear once in your city. If you already have a Unique card in your city, you cannot play a second copy of it. The card name has a small banner underneath it indicating whether it is Common or Unique, so check before you play.

walkthrough.title: Card Types: Tan Traveler
walkthrough.text: Tan Traveler cards give you a one-time benefit the moment you play them into your city. You might gain resources, draw cards, or score points immediately. After the effect resolves, the card sits in your city contributing its point value at game end but does not trigger again. Think of Travelers as instant rewards that also count toward your fifteen-card city.

walkthrough.title: Card Types: Green Production
walkthrough.text: Green Production cards activate when you play them and again whenever you Prepare for Season during spring and autumn. These are your engine cards. They generate resources, draw cards, or grant points each time they fire. The more Production cards you have, the bigger the payoff when you trigger a Prepare for Season. Getting Production cards down early maximizes the number of times they activate.

walkthrough.title: Card Types: Red Destination
walkthrough.text: Red Destination cards create new worker placement spots in your city. Once a Destination card is in your city, you can send one of your own workers there on a future turn to gain its benefit. Some Destinations are open, meaning other players can also send a worker there, but you get a bonus when they do. Destination cards essentially expand the board with personal locations only you control.

walkthrough.title: Card Types: Blue Governance
walkthrough.text: Blue Governance cards give you ongoing conditional bonuses. For example, a Governance card might grant you a point token every time you play a Construction, or give you a resource discount on certain card types. These rewards trigger passively whenever the condition is met during the game. Governance cards reward you for doing things you were already planning to do, so look for ones that match your strategy.

walkthrough.title: Card Types: Purple Prosperity
walkthrough.text: Purple Prosperity cards score bonus victory points at the end of the game based on conditions in your city. For example, a Prosperity card might award points for each pair of Critters and Constructions you have. These do nothing during the game but can be worth a huge number of points in final scoring. Play them early if possible so they do not clog your hand, but make sure you can actually meet their conditions.

walkthrough.title: Action: Prepare for Season
walkthrough.text: When you have no workers left to place or you want to move on, you can Prepare for Season. This advances you to the next season and does three things. First, you bring all your deployed workers back from the board and your city Destinations. Second, you gain new workers from the Ever Tree. Third, you activate all your Green Production cards. In spring you gain one worker and activate production. In summer you gain one worker and draw two cards from the Meadow. In autumn you gain two workers and activate production.

walkthrough.title: Season Details
walkthrough.text: Spring gives you one new worker plus Production activation. Summer gives you one new worker plus you draw two cards from the Meadow without replacing them until after your selection. Autumn gives you two new workers plus Production activation. Note that summer does not trigger Production cards, only spring and autumn do. After autumn, you continue playing until all your workers are placed, then you are done for the game.

walkthrough.title: Independent Season Advancement
walkthrough.text: A critical concept: players advance seasons independently. You might prepare for summer on your turn while another player stays in spring for several more turns. This is perfectly normal and a key strategic decision. Preparing for Season early gets you more workers sooner, but you might miss out on playing cards or gathering resources first. Waiting too long means your opponents have more workers out while you are still catching up.

walkthrough.title: Journey
walkthrough.text: Once you have prepared for autumn, you unlock a special ability called Journey. Instead of placing a worker on a board location, you can send a worker on a Journey by discarding cards from your hand. Place your worker on one of the Journey spots at the bottom of the board and discard the required number of cards. You score two, three, four, or five victory points depending on the spot. Journey is a great way to convert extra cards into points in the final stretch of the game.

walkthrough.title: Claiming Events
walkthrough.text: You can claim an Event as your action on a turn. For Basic Events, place a worker on the Event tile to claim it. For Special Events on the Ever Tree, you need to have the required cards in your city. Each Event can only be claimed by one player. Check the conditions printed on the Event, and if you qualify, take it. Events are worth bonus points at the end of the game and can be the difference between winning and losing.

walkthrough.title: Hand Limit
walkthrough.text: Your hand limit is eight cards at all times. If you ever have more than eight cards in hand, you must immediately discard down to eight. This can happen when you draw cards from various sources. Be mindful of your hand size, especially when triggering draw abilities. Cards stuck in your hand are worth zero points, so play them or use them for Journey rather than hoarding.

walkthrough.title: City Limit
walkthrough.text: Your city has a maximum capacity of fifteen cards. Once you have fifteen cards in your city, you cannot play any more. This means every card slot is precious. Avoid filling your city with low-value cards early in the game when you could save those slots for higher-value plays later. Plan your city composition carefully to maximize your final score.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends when all players have prepared for autumn and placed all their workers. Players may finish at different times since seasons are independent. Once everyone is done, add up your score. Count the point value printed on every card in your city. Add any point tokens you earned during the game. Add points from Prosperity cards. Add Journey points. Add Event bonuses. The player with the highest total wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most Events claimed wins.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Everdell
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first season with three example players. Each player will take different actions so you can see how the game flows. Follow along with the moves even if you have a different number of players at your table. Ready to build your woodland city? Let's go.

walkthrough.title: Starting Position
walkthrough.text: Everyone begins in winter with two workers and five cards in hand. Take a moment to look at your cards. You will see a mix of Critters and Constructions with different resource costs. Notice the color of each card: tan, green, red, blue, or purple. Also check if any of your Critters have a linked Construction icon in the upper left. Those connections will become very important.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Place a Worker for Resources
walkthrough.text: Player 1, let's start simple. You need resources to play cards, so place one of your two workers on a location that gives you twigs and resin. Pick up the matching resources from the supply. Nice. You have taken your first action in Everdell. You have one worker left this season.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Place a Worker for Berries
walkthrough.text: Player 2, you have a couple of Critter cards in your hand that cost berries. Place your worker on a location that grants berries. Collect your berries from the supply. Berries are how you pay for Critters, so stocking up now gives you options to play creatures soon.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Play a Card
walkthrough.text: Player 3, you started with a low-cost Construction in your hand that only needs one twig and one resin. Since you already have those resources from somewhere, let's play it right away. Pay the twig and resin back to the supply and place the Construction card in front of you. This is the start of your city! Check if this Construction has a linked Critter, because you might be able to play that Critter for free later.

walkthrough.title: Why Playing Early Matters
walkthrough.text: Player 3 made a great move. Getting a card into your city early, especially a Green Production card, means it will activate more times over the course of the game. Every time you Prepare for Season in spring or autumn, all your Production cards fire. A card played in winter could activate twice, while one played in autumn activates zero times. Timing is everything.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Play a Construction
walkthrough.text: Pay the cost, place it in your city. Now look at that Construction carefully. Does it have a small Critter icon in the bottom right corner? If so, that means there is a Critter card in the deck that you could play for free later, as long as you have this Construction in your city.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Play a Critter
walkthrough.text: Player 2, you have berries now. Play a Critter from your hand by paying its berry cost. Place it in your city next to any other cards you have. Critters and Constructions sit side by side in your city, and their order does not matter. Just make sure you can tell which ones are yours.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: The Mistake — Missing a Free Critter
walkthrough.text: Player 3, you have a Farm in your city from earlier. You also just drew a Husband card, and the Farm's linked Critter is the Husband. Instead of checking for the free play, Player 3 spends three berries to play the Husband the normal way. That is three berries wasted! Always check your Constructions for linked Critter icons before paying full price. Those free plays are some of the best value in the game.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check for Free Critter Plays
walkthrough.text: Before you pay berries for any Critter, scan your city for a matching linked Construction. The Critter card shows which Construction it pairs with in the upper left corner. If you have that Construction and have not already used its free Critter ability, play the Critter for free. Mark the Construction so you remember you have used it. Missing free plays is the number one beginner mistake in Everdell.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Prepare for Spring
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has used both workers and played a card. With no workers left, Player 1 decides to Prepare for Season. They advance to spring, which means three things happen. All deployed workers come back from the board. Player 1 gains one new worker from the Ever Tree, bringing their total to three. And all Green Production cards in their city activate. If Player 1 has a Production card that grants a twig, they collect a twig right now.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: The Mistake — Filling the City Too Fast
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has been playing every card they can afford, filling their city rapidly with low-cost, low-point cards. They are at ten cards already and it is only spring. The problem? With fifteen cards maximum, Player 2 only has five slots left for the rest of the game. Those remaining slots need to hold high-value cards, Prosperity scorers, and key combo pieces. By filling up early with low-value filler, Player 2 is locking themselves out of better plays later.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Protect Your City Slots
walkthrough.text: Your city has exactly fifteen slots, and that limit is real. Every one-point or two-point card you play now is a slot you cannot use for a five-point card later. Think of your city like a fifteen-room hotel. Do not fill it with budget guests when the VIPs are arriving soon. Be selective, especially in winter and spring. Save slots for the powerful cards you will find as the game develops.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Use the Meadow
walkthrough.text: Player 3 looks at their hand and does not see anything great to play. But wait, the Meadow has eight face-up cards available to everyone. Player 3 spots a perfect Green Production card in the Meadow that synergizes with their city. They pay its cost and play it directly from the Meadow instead of their hand. The Meadow is immediately refilled with a new card from the deck.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: The Meadow Is Your Second Hand
walkthrough.text: New players often forget about the Meadow and only play from their hand. The Meadow gives you access to eight additional cards every turn. Scan it regularly, especially before drawing more cards into your hand. Sometimes the perfect card is sitting in the Meadow right now, and you do not need to waste an action drawing. Think of the Meadow as a public hand that everyone can shop from.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: The Mistake — Waiting Too Long to Prepare
walkthrough.text: Player 2 still has not prepared for spring while Player 1 is already there with three workers. Player 2 keeps trying to squeeze one more card play out of winter. Meanwhile, Player 1 is placing three workers per round while Player 2 is stuck with none. The workers sitting on the Ever Tree are doing nothing for you. Every turn you delay preparing for season is a turn those extra workers sit idle.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Wait Too Long to Prepare
walkthrough.text: Preparing for Season is not just about activating Production cards. It is about getting your workers back and gaining new ones. Delaying too long means your opponents have more workers on the board claiming the best spots while you are stuck. A good rule of thumb: prepare once you have used all your available workers and cannot play any more useful cards. Do not hoard the current season just because you can.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: The Mistake — Ignoring Hand Limit
walkthrough.text: Player 1 uses a worker on a card draw location and gains three new cards. But they already had seven cards in hand, bringing the total to ten. The hand limit is eight, so Player 1 must immediately discard two cards. They agonize over which to toss and end up discarding a card they needed. If Player 1 had played a card from hand first, they would have had room for the draws.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Manage Your Hand Size
walkthrough.text: The hand limit of eight is strict and unforgiving. Before drawing cards, count your hand. If you are at seven, you can only keep one new card from a draw-three ability. Play or discard cards before drawing to make room. Cards forced out of your hand are wasted actions. Plan your draws for when your hand is lean, not when it is already full.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues clockwise with each player taking one action per turn. Place Workers to gather resources, Play Cards to build your city, and Prepare for Season when you are ready to advance. As you move through spring and into summer, you will gain more workers and your Production engine will grow. Keep scanning the Meadow, check for free Critter plays, and watch your hand and city limits.

walkthrough.title: The Final Push: Autumn
walkthrough.text: This is also when Journey unlocks. If you have cards stuck in your hand that you cannot play, send workers on Journeys to convert those cards into points. Autumn is your last chance to claim Events, fill your city with high-value cards, and squeeze every last point out of your engine.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen all three actions in play, learned about free Critter plays, watched players make common mistakes, and discovered how the season system works. Remember: check for free Critter links, protect your city slots, use the Meadow, do not delay preparing for season, and manage your hand limit. You are ready to build your own woodland city.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three resources to help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and the most common pitfalls to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to card types, resources, and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Public market everyone can shop from

summary.title: Player 2: The Mistake — Waiting Too Long to Prepare
bullets: Player 2 delays Prepare for Season while Player 1 has 3 workers
Workers on Ever Tree are doing nothing for you
Opponents claim the best spots while you're stuck

summary.title: Lesson: Don't Wait Too Long to Prepare
bullets: Preparing = workers back + new workers + Production
Delaying too long = opponents outpace you with more workers
Prepare once all workers are placed and no useful cards to play

summary.title: Player 1: The Mistake — Ignoring Hand Limit
bullets: Player 1 draws 3 cards at 7 in hand → forced to discard 2
Loses a needed card due to poor hand management
Should have played cards first to make room

summary.title: Lesson: Manage Your Hand Size
bullets: Hand limit = 8, strictly enforced
Count hand before drawing — play/discard first to make room
Forced discards = wasted actions

summary.title: Continue Playing
bullets: Clockwise turns: Place Worker, Play Card, or Prepare for Season
Engine grows as you move through spring → summer → autumn
Keep checking Meadow, free Critter links, hand/city limits

summary.title: The Final Push: Autumn
bullets: Autumn: +2 workers, final Production activation, Journey unlocks
Journey = discard cards for 2-5 VP per worker
Last chance for Events, high-value city fills, point squeezing

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: All 3 actions practiced, common mistakes demonstrated
Key lessons: free Critter plays, city slots, Meadow, season timing, hand limit
Ready to build your own woodland city

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for card types and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new players make. First, missing free Critter plays through linked Constructions. Always scan your city before paying berries. Second, filling your city to fifteen cards with low-value filler before the good cards show up. Third, waiting too long to Prepare for Season and falling behind on workers. Fourth, ignoring the Meadow and only playing from your hand. Fifth, forgetting the hand limit of eight and losing good cards to forced discards. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Early Game Priorities
walkthrough.text: In winter and early spring, your goals are simple: get Green Production cards into your city as fast as possible. Production cards that generate resources on Prepare for Season are the engine of your game. A card played in winter can activate twice, once in spring and once in autumn. A card played in summer only activates once in autumn. So every turn you delay playing a Production card, you lose one activation forever. Prioritize cheap Green cards first, then build around them.

walkthrough.title: Resource Management
walkthrough.text: Everdell has four resources and they serve different purposes. Twigs, resin, and pebbles pay for Constructions. Berries pay for Critters. A common mistake is hoarding one resource type while neglecting others. Keep a balanced supply so you have options. Pebbles are the scarcest resource on the board, so when you see a pebble location open, strongly consider grabbing it. Also remember that free Critter plays save berries, which is effectively like gaining resources.

walkthrough.title: Construction-Critter Pairings
walkthrough.text: It is essentially getting two cards for the price of one. When you draw a Construction, immediately check if you have or could find its linked Critter. When you draw a Critter, check if the linked Construction is in the Meadow or already in your city. Planning around these pairings can save you six or more berries over the course of a game, which is a massive advantage.

walkthrough.title: When to Prepare for Season
walkthrough.text: Knowing when to advance seasons is one of the most important skills in Everdell. A good rule of thumb: prepare once all your workers are deployed and you cannot play a card you actually want to play. Do not prepare just because you can, and do not wait forever hoping for a perfect moment. Watch what your opponents are doing. If they have already advanced and have more workers out than you, you are falling behind. The workers on the Ever Tree are doing nothing for you until you claim them.

walkthrough.title: City Slot Management
walkthrough.text: With only fifteen slots in your city, every card matters. In the early game, play cards that generate ongoing value: Production cards, Destination cards, and Governance cards. In the mid-game, start looking for Prosperity cards that will score big at the end. In the late game, fill remaining slots with whatever gives you the most points. A good target is to have twelve to thirteen cards by the time you reach autumn, leaving two to three slots for high-value final plays.

walkthrough.title: Events Are Free Points
walkthrough.text: Events are one of the most overlooked scoring opportunities in the game. Basic Events typically require simple conditions like having certain card types in your city. Special Events on the Ever Tree require specific card combinations. Because claiming an Event costs you an action and sometimes a worker, plan for them ahead of time. Check the available Events at the start of the game and keep them in mind as you build your city. An Event worth three to five points for a single action is an excellent trade.

walkthrough.title: Journey and Endgame Cleanup
walkthrough.text: Once you prepare for autumn, Journey becomes available. This is your safety valve for cards stuck in your hand. Instead of letting those cards score zero points, discard them to send workers on Journeys worth two to five victory points each. Plan your autumn to have a few extra cards in hand specifically for Journey. Also scan the Meadow one last time for any high-point cards you can afford. The difference between a good and great final score often comes down to how efficiently you use your last few workers.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Missing free Critter plays via linked Constructions
Filling 15-card city with low-value filler too early
Waiting too long to Prepare for Season — falling behind on workers
Ignoring the Meadow — 8 free card options every turn
Forgetting hand limit of 8 — forced discards waste actions

walkthrough.title: The Production Engine
walkthrough.text: The most consistent strategy in Everdell revolves around Green Production cards. The idea is to fill your city with Production cards as early as possible so they fire multiple times during Prepare for Season. A General Store that grants a berry each activation will give you three berries over the game if played in winter. Pair it with other Production cards that generate twigs or resin, and you create a self-fueling engine. The key is getting three or four Production cards down before your first Prepare for Season in spring.

walkthrough.title: Destination Control
walkthrough.text: Red Destination cards are quietly among the strongest in the game. Each one adds a worker placement spot that only you can use, effectively giving you access to locations your opponents cannot block. A city with three or four Destinations means you always have productive places to send workers, even when the board is crowded. Look for Destinations that grant resources you need or that synergize with your Governance cards. In a tight game, having guaranteed worker placement spots is a huge advantage.

walkthrough.title: Prosperity Card Timing
walkthrough.text: Purple Prosperity cards score points at game end based on what is in your city, but they contribute nothing during the game. The optimal time to play them is mid-game, around summer. Playing them too early wastes a city slot on a card that does nothing yet. Playing them too late means you might not be able to meet their conditions. If a Prosperity card rewards you for having pairs of Critters and Constructions, play it in summer and then spend autumn filling your city with pairs.

walkthrough.title: The Free Critter Chain
walkthrough.text: Advanced players plan their entire game around Construction-Critter pairings. The strategy works like this: play a Construction, then play its linked Critter for free, saving the full berry cost. With three to four pairings over the game, you save nine to twelve berries worth of resources. To maximize this, prioritize Constructions whose linked Critters are either in your hand, in the Meadow, or likely to show up. Build the Construction first, then hunt for the Critter. This is essentially getting two cards in your city for the price of one.

walkthrough.title: Meadow Manipulation
walkthrough.text: The Meadow is a shared resource that refreshes every time someone takes a card. Advanced players pay close attention to what is in the Meadow and time their actions to grab the best cards before opponents. If you see a card in the Meadow that links to a Construction in your city, grab it immediately. If you see a card your opponent needs, consider whether taking it is worth your action even if it is suboptimal for you. The Meadow refresh during summer Prepare for Season is especially important, as you get to draw two cards from it.

walkthrough.title: Worker Tempo
walkthrough.text: Understanding worker tempo separates good players from great ones. You start with two workers and gain four more over three season advances, for a total of six. The question is not just where to place workers, but when to advance seasons. Advancing early gives you more total worker placements because you have more workers available sooner. But advancing too early means you miss out on critical card plays. The sweet spot is to advance when your Production cards will generate more value than the actions you would give up by not placing workers this season.

walkthrough.title: Event Planning
walkthrough.text: Events are worth three to six points each and can be planned for from the start of the game. At setup, read all four Special Events and four Basic Events. Identify which ones align with cards in your starting hand or strategy. Then build toward claiming two or three Events over the course of the game. Claiming Events is most efficient in autumn when you have the most workers and your city is nearly complete. An overlooked Event is essentially free points left on the table.

walkthrough.title: Governance Synergies
walkthrough.text: Blue Governance cards provide ongoing bonuses that compound over time. The key is to play them before the actions they reward. A Governance card that grants a point token every time you play a Construction is most valuable when played in winter or spring, before you play most of your Constructions. Stacking two Governance cards that trigger on the same condition creates powerful double bonuses. Look for Governance cards that align with what you are already planning to do, and play them as early as possible to maximize their triggers.

walkthrough.title: Optimizing Journey
walkthrough.text: Journey is not just a consolation prize for leftover cards. Advanced players deliberately hold cards in hand for autumn Journey actions. Each Journey spot requires discarding a specific number of cards for two to five points. If you can fill two Journey spots for a total of seven to nine points, that rivals the value of playing cards into your city. Plan your autumn hand size to have four to six cards available for Journey. This also means being strategic about what you draw in summer, since those two Meadow cards can fuel your Journey.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Board State
walkthrough.text: The highest-level Everdell play involves reading your opponents' cities and adapting. If you see an opponent building toward a specific Event, consider claiming it first. If someone is heavily invested in Constructions, they probably have Critters to pair. Watch which board locations are congested and plan to use them early in the round before they fill up. If pebble locations are always contested, consider cards that generate pebbles instead. The best players win not just by executing their own plan, but by understanding what everyone else is trying to do.

entries.definition: One of three possible actions. Take one available worker and place it on an open location on the board, a Forest card, or a Destination card in your city. Immediately gain the location's reward. Worker stays until you Prepare for Season.

One of three possible actions. Choose a card from your hand or the Meadow, pay its resource cost, and place it into your city. Your city has a maximum of fifteen cards. You may also play a linked Critter for free if its paired Construction is in your city.

One of three possible actions. Advance to the next season, recall all deployed workers, gain new workers from the Ever Tree, and activate Green Production cards (spring and autumn only). Summer grants two Meadow card draws instead of Production activation.

Available only after preparing for autumn. Instead of placing a worker on a board location, send it to a Journey spot by discarding cards from your hand. Scores two, three, four, or five victory points depending on the spot chosen.
Card type with a tan color band. Grants a one-time benefit when played into your city, such as resources, cards, or points. Does not activate again. Provides base victory points at game end.

Card type with a green color band. Activates when played and again during Prepare for Season in spring and autumn. Generates resources, cards, or points each activation. The engine core of Everdell. Play as early as possible.

Card type with a red color band. Creates a new worker placement spot in your city. Only the owner can use closed Destinations. Open Destinations allow other players to visit, granting the owner a bonus when they do.

Card type with a blue color band. Provides ongoing conditional bonuses that trigger whenever a specific condition is met during the game. Examples include gaining a point token when playing a Construction or gaining a resource when playing a Critter.

Card type with a purple color band. Scores bonus victory points at the end of the game based on conditions in your city. Does nothing during the game. Best played in mid-game to allow time to meet conditions.

One of two card categories. Constructions represent buildings and structures. They cost twigs, resin, and pebbles. Many Constructions have a linked Critter that can be played for free once the Construction is in your city.

One of two card categories. Critters represent woodland animals. They cost berries. Many Critters have a linked Construction, and if that Construction is already in your city, you can play the Critter for free without paying berries.
A card marked as Common on its banner. You may have multiple copies of a Common card in your city. There is no limit to how many Common cards of the same name you can play.
A card marked as Unique on its banner. You may only have one copy of a Unique card in your city. If you already have it, you cannot play a second copy.

A Critter card that is paired with a specific Construction. If the linked Construction is in your city and has not already been used for a free play, you can play the Critter without paying its berry cost. Each Construction can only provide one free Critter play.
One of four resource types. Shaped like small sticks. Primarily used to pay for Construction cards. Commonly available from basic board locations and Forest cards.
One of four resource types. Shaped like amber drops. Used to pay for Construction cards, often alongside twigs. Available from board locations and some Production cards.
One of four resource types. Shaped like small stones. The scarcest resource on the board. Used for higher-cost Constructions. Pebble locations are often contested, so grab them when open.
One of four resource types. Round purple tokens. Used to pay for Critter cards. Can be saved by using free Critter plays through linked Constructions.
Tokens in values of one, three, and five victory points. Earned during the game from card abilities, Governance triggers, and other effects. Added to final score at game end.

A row of eight face-up cards beside the board available to all players. Cards can be played directly from the Meadow by paying their resource cost. When a card is taken, it is immediately replaced from the deck.

The three-dimensional cardboard tree centerpiece on the board. Holds Special Event cards on its branches and tracks which workers are unlocked in each season. Workers are claimed from the tree when preparing for season.
Location tiles placed on the forest path on the board during setup. Number used depends on player count. Provide additional worker placement options with unique resource and card combinations.

One of four event tiles placed along the bottom of the board. Each has a simple condition to meet. Claimed by placing a worker on it. Each Basic Event can only be claimed by one player. Worth bonus VP at game end.

One of four event cards placed on the Ever Tree branches. Require specific card combinations in your city to claim. More difficult conditions but typically higher point rewards than Basic Events. Each can only be claimed by one player.
The starting season. All players begin in winter with two workers and five cards. No special abilities are unlocked yet. Focus on gathering resources and playing early cards to build your engine.
The second season. When you Prepare for Spring, you recall all workers, gain one new worker from the Ever Tree, and activate all Green Production cards in your city. Total workers available: three.

The third season. When you Prepare for Summer, you recall all workers, gain one new worker, and draw two cards from the Meadow. Production cards do NOT activate in summer. Total workers available: four.

The final season. When you Prepare for Autumn, you recall all workers, gain two new workers, and activate all Green Production cards. Journey is now unlocked. Total workers available: six. Game ends when all autumn workers are placed.

Your personal play area where you place cards. Maximum capacity of fifteen cards. Cards in your city include Constructions and Critters of all five color types. Your city is the primary source of victory points at game end.
The maximum of fifteen cards in your city. Once you reach fifteen, you cannot play any more cards. Every slot is valuable, so avoid filling your city with low-point cards early in the game.
The maximum of eight cards in your hand at all times. If you ever exceed eight, you must immediately discard down. Plan your draws carefully to avoid losing good cards to forced discards.

Wooden meeples used to claim locations on the board and in cities. Each player has six total: two available at start, one gained in spring, one in summer, and two in autumn. Workers are recalled when you Prepare for Season.

A Red Destination card that allows other players to send their workers there. When an opponent visits your open Destination, you receive a bonus printed on the card. This gives you passive income from opponents' actions.
A Red Destination card that only you can use. Other players cannot send workers there. Provides a guaranteed personal location that cannot be blocked.
A board location marked with an open circle that holds only one worker at a time. Once occupied, no other worker can go there until it clears when its owner Prepares for Season.
A board location marked with a larger circle that can hold multiple workers simultaneously. Any number of players can place workers here.

When you Prepare for Season in spring or autumn, all Green Production cards in your city activate, granting their printed benefits. Summer does not trigger Production. The more Production cards you have, the bigger the payoff.
The victory point value printed on each card in your city. Added to your total at game end. Ranges from zero to five or more depending on the card. One of the primary scoring categories.
All point tokens collected during the game from card abilities, Governance bonuses, and other effects are added to your final score. Available in denominations of one, three, and five.

Purple Prosperity cards score bonus victory points at game end based on conditions in your city. They contribute nothing during the game but can be worth significant points if conditions are well met.
Victory points earned by sending workers on Journeys in autumn. Each Journey spot awards two, three, four, or five points in exchange for discarding cards from your hand.
Victory points from claimed Basic Events and Special Events. Each event is worth bonus points at game end. Events are tiebreakers if final scores are equal.
